es.meaning: Once upon a time — the fixed opener of fairy tales and stories.
es.example: Había una vez una princesa que vivía en un castillo.
es.exampleTranslation: Once upon a time there was a princess who lived in a castle.
es.regionNote: The archaic variant 'Érase una vez' (with fossilised enclitic -se) is equally canonical, especially in Spain and in print. Reviewer to confirm which form is LatAm-default for the headword.

it.meaning: Once upon a time — the fixed opener of fairy tales and stories.
it.example: C'era una volta un re che aveva tre figlie.
it.exampleTranslation: Once upon a time there was a king who had three daughters.

bridge.note: Identical openers hiding the deepest grammar split between the two languages: the existential. Spanish builds 'there was' on haber (había); Italian builds it on esserci (c'era) — and although avere is haber's direct cognate, Italian never uses it existentially. Bonus false friend: volta is the cognate of Spanish vuelta ('turn'), not of vez — Italian literally counts occasions in turns, the same volta that was just flipped in entry 0028's frittata.

interference.es_to_it: The haber→avere cognate pull produces 'aveva una volta' or 'ha molte cose' for existentials. Italian existentials take esserci: c'è, c'era. If you can say c'era una volta, you already own the pattern — lean on it.
interference.it_to_es: Italians reach for essere and produce 'era una vez' — half-camouflaged by the archaic 'Érase una vez', so it slips by, but productive Spanish existentials take haber: hay, había.

review.notes: Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14 (as 'Habia una vez / cera una volta'; forms normalised to 'Había' and 'C'era'). CLASSIFICATION: typed idiom, not formula — the schema defines formula as a fixed SOCIAL phrase tied to an occasion (buon anno), which is why formulas require pins. A narrative opener has no occasion; it is a fixed discourse idiom. Reviewer may overrule.

cue: Once upon a time… (opening a story)
